title: The Original Bonfire of the Vanities - History Today
highlights: February 1497, the Piagnoni of Florence set sin ablaze in the original ‘bonfire of the vanities’  ...    the streets of Florence in the 1490s, shouting abuse at the visibly impious: drunks, gamblers, women. They were called ‘pinzocheroni’, too: bigots. They, like the city, were under the sway of Girolamo Savonarola, a Dominican friar who  ...    Carnival in Florence was a time of wantonness and riot; under Savonarola it became an orderly ritual of destruction, culminating on 7 February 1497 with what is remembered as the bonfire of the vanities. Piagnoni went door to door demanding the surrender of sinful objects. One witness listed what they took: ‘lascivious pictures … women’s hats, mirrors, wigs, dolls, perfumes, pictures in intarsia, sculptures, cupids, playing cards, dice  ...    Everything was brought to the Piazza della Signoria, where a great tiered wooden edifice had been built, somewhat like a pyramid, filled with brushwood and broom.
